skill.name: python-performance-optimization
skill.description: Profile and optimize Python applications with CPU profiling, memory profiling, benchmarking, caching, async I/O, and database tuning guidance.
skill.summary: Find Python bottlenecks and apply measured performance improvements.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution alerts, but review shows they are Markdown code fences and example profiler commands, not executable skill code. The skill is documentation-only, with low residual risk from copied examples that run local profilers, open local files, or call httpbin.org for I/O benchmarking.

low_findings.title: Example Commands Profile Local Processes
low_findings.description: The py-spy examples inspect a local process by PID and can expose stack information if a user runs them against sensitive production services. This is legitimate performance tooling, and no evidence shows the skill executes these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line-numbered context shows explicit py-spy commands against a PID. The risk depends on a user manually running the documented command, so severity remains low.

low_findings.title: Example HTTP Benchmark Uses External Service
low_findings.description: The async I/O example imports requests and aiohttp and calls https://httpbin.org/delay/1. This is a benign timing demonstration, but copied code would make outbound network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs and HTTP client calls are visible in the referenced lines. They target a public test endpoint and do not include credentials or exfiltration logic.

low_findings.title: Examples Open Local Files and Databases
low_findings.description: The SQLite and iterator examples open example.db or a caller-provided filename. This is normal optimization guidance, but users should test with non-sensitive data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly demonstrate local file and database access. They are not hidden behavior because SKILL.md is the only file and contains documentation examples only.

low_findings.title: Static Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown fenced code blocks and sample snippets. No standalone script, installer, or automatic command runner was found in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows fenced code examples rather than executable Ruby backticks. The package contains only SKILL.md, so there is no execution surface outside user-copied examples.

content.value_statement: Slow Python services and data jobs are hard to fix without measurements. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through profiling, benchmarking, memory analysis, and targeted optimization.
actual_capabilities: Explains CPU profiling with cProfile, pstats, line_profiler, and py-spy.
Shows memory profiling patterns with memory_profiler, tracemalloc, generators, and weak references.
Compares common Python optimization patterns for loops, comprehensions, caching, and data structures.
Covers async I/O, multiprocessing, batch database writes, and query planning examples.
Provides benchmarking practices with timeit, custom decorators, and pytest-benchmark.
limitations: It is a guidance file and does not run profiling tools by itself.
Examples need adaptation before use in production systems.
It does not replace application-specific load testing or observability data.
Some examples require optional packages such as py-spy, line-profiler, or pytest-benchmark.

use_cases.title: Reduce API Latency
use_cases.description: Profile slow request paths, identify hot functions, and choose focused code or database changes.

use_cases.title: Speed Up Data Processing
use_cases.description: Compare loops, comprehensions, generators, multiprocessing, and caching for large Python workloads.

use_cases.title: Control Memory Growth
use_cases.description: Use memory profiling, tracemalloc snapshots, iterators, and weak references to find avoidable allocation growth.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Starting Point
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me profile a slow Python function. Explain which profiler to start with and what results to capture.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret Profile Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to interpret my Python profiling results. Identify likely bottlenecks and suggest the safest next measurements.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Workload
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to optimize a Python data-processing workload. Compare algorithmic, memory, caching, and parallel execution options.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Performance Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a performance review plan for a production Python service, including CPU, memory, I/O, database, and regression benchmarks.

output_examples.input: A Python API endpoint is slow under normal traffic.
output_examples.output: A profiling plan that starts with request timing, uses cProfile or py-spy, ranks hot paths, and validates each change with repeatable benchmarks.

output_examples.input: A batch job uses too much memory on large files.
output_examples.output: A memory review that checks allocations, replaces full-list loading with iterators, and compares peak memory before and after changes.

output_examples.input: Database inserts are slowing a Python import task.
output_examples.output: A database optimization checklist covering batch writes, transaction scope, indexes, query plans, and benchmark criteria.

best_practices: Measure first, then optimize the hottest path with the smallest clear change.
Benchmark before and after each change with representative input sizes.
Use production-like data only when access controls and privacy rules allow it.
anti_patterns: Do not rewrite code for speed before identifying a measured bottleneck.
Do not compare benchmarks with different inputs, environments, or cache states.
Do not run profiler examples against sensitive production processes without approval.

faq.question: Does this skill run profiling tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that a user can adapt and run manually.

faq.question: Which profiler should I start with?
faq.answer: Start with cProfile for CPU bottlenecks, then use line_profiler or py-spy when you need deeper detail.

faq.question: Can it help with memory leaks?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains memory_profiler, tracemalloc snapshots, generators, and weak references for memory analysis.

faq.question: Does it cover database performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for batch inserts, indexes, query planning, and selecting only needed columns.

faq.question: Is it useful for async Python applications?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares synchronous requests with aiohttp-based async I/O for I/O-bound workloads.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this for code reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. They can use it to structure profiling reviews and suggest measured optimization steps.
